coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional porous organic polymer: Selective detecting of Fe (III) ion and reversible capturing iodine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Ruihan
Qiu, Na
Kong, Xiangfei
Hu, Zhenguang
Zhong, Fuxin
Zhang, Peipei
Tan, Haijun
coredata.dc:description: Interest in functional porous organic polymer has rapidly grown over the years due to significant advances in sensing, catalysis, adsorption and separation applications. Here, a novel triazine porous organic polymer (CTF-DB-MA) containing bithiophene was synthesized by using 5,5′ −dibromo-2,2′ −bithiophene and melamine as building blocks. This tailor-made polymer has high affinity and adsorption capacity for iodine due to its effective electron rich N/S adsorption sites, π − conjugated structure, and layered porous structure. CTF-DB-MA displays an excellent reversible iodine uptake in iodine vapor and iodine n-hexane solution, and the maximum adsorption amounts were 2255.81 mg/g and 230.09 mg/g, respectively. Furthermore, CTF-DB-MA exhibits nice detection sensitivity towards Fe3+ via fluorescence quenching, with a limit of detection of 0.25 μM. CTF-DB-MA as multifunctional material can simultaneously achieve Fe3+ ion detection and iodine capture. The synergic functionality of the novel polymer makes them promising materials to the environment and public health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent triazine frameworks
Fe3+ detection
Iodine adsorption
Electron-rich sorption site

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra. (b) TGA plots for CTF-DB-MA. (c) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K of CTF-DB-MA (d) DFT pore size distribution for CTF-DB-MA. (e) SEM of CTF-DB-MA.

(a) Fluorescence spectral responses of the CTF-DB-MA in Diox solution upon addition of Al3+、Ba2+、Ca2+、Cd2+、Co2+、Cr2+、Cu2+、Fe2+、Fe3+、Hg2+、K+、Mg2+、Mn2+、Na+、Ni2+、Pb2+、Zn2+ (λex = 394 nm). (b) Competitive selectivity of CTF-DB-MA toward Fe3+ ion in the presence of other metal ions under an excitation of 394 nm. (c) Fluorescence spectra of the CTF-DB-MA in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (d) Linear fitting curve of the CTF-DB-MA with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ ions.

(a) XPS full-scan spectra of CTF-DB-MA and CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+. (b) Fe 2p XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+. (c) N 1 s XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA. (d)N 1 s XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+. (e) S 2p XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA. (f) S 2p XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+.
(a) Iodine vapor uptake of CTF-DB-MA. (b) Recycle iodine capture experiments for CTF-DB-MA.
(a) Adsorption amount of iodine in n-hexane solution. (b) Removal rate over time of iodine in n-hexane solution. (c) Langmuir models of CTF-DB-MA. (d) Freundlich models of CTF-DB-MA.
The XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA before and after adsorption of iodine vapor and I3 −.

The XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA before and after adsorption of iodine vapor and I3 −(a). The I 3d spectra of I2@ CTF-DB-MA (b) and I3 −@ CTF-DB-MA (c). The N 1 s spectra of CTF-DB-MA (d), I2@ CTF-DB-MA (e), and I3 −@ CTF-DB-MA (f).
Raman spectra of CTF-DB-MA before and after adsorption of iodine vapor (a) and I3 − (b).
Synthesis of CTF-CAR by catalyst-free Schiff-base polymerization.
Kinetic model of adsorption of iodine vapor by CTF-DB-MA.
Langmuir and Freundlich isothermal model parameters of CTF-DB-MA adsorption of iodine.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional porous organic polymer: Selective detecting of Fe (III) ion and reversible capturing iodine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, China 541004

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, China 541004

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, China 541004.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: R.T and N.Q contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Interest in functional porous organic polymer has rapidly grown over the years due to significant advances in sensing, catalysis, adsorption and separation applications. Here, a novel triazine porous organic polymer (CTF-DB-MA) containing bithiophene was synthesized by using 5,5′ −dibromo-2,2′ −bithiophene and melamine as building blocks. This tailor-made polymer has high affinity and adsorption capacity for iodine due to its effective electron rich N/S adsorption sites, π − conjugated structure, and layered porous structure. CTF-DB-MA displays an excellent reversible iodine uptake in iodine vapor and iodine n-hexane solution, and the maximum adsorption amounts were 2255.81 mg/g and 230.09 mg/g, respectively. Furthermore, CTF-DB-MA exhibits nice detection sensitivity towards Fe3+ via fluorescence quenching, with a limit of detection of 0.25 μM. CTF-DB-MA as multifunctional material can simultaneously achieve Fe3+ ion detection and iodine capture. The synergic functionality of the novel polymer makes them promising materials to the environment and public health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nuclear energy, as a clean and efficient form of energy, has the advantages of high energy density and extremely low carbon emissions [1–5]. However, the production process of nuclear energy involves high-risk technical links [6–9]. Once an accident occurs, such as a nuclear leak or explosion, it is possible to release high-risk radioactive isotopes [10,11]. Among them, 129I and 131I are notable for their extremely long half-lives (129I is 1.57 × 107 years) [12,13]. Once absorbed by the body, radioactive iodine can cause various thyroid diseases and even cancer [14], it is considered a dangerous pollutant even in extremely low concentrations [6]. Therefore, to make better use of nuclear energy resources, the development of an efficient, reusable and environmentally friendly iodine capture material is of great significance for building safe nuclear energy.

Adsorption method is considered an effective iodine capture method due to its low cost, applicability, and environmental friendliness [15]. There are currently various adsorbents used for the capture of iodine, including inorganic porous materials, organic porous materials, and inorganic organic hybrid porous materials [16–21]. Among them, covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) are considered one of the ideal iodine capture materials due to their controllable design, multiple preparation methods, unique structure, large specific surface area, high porosity, high stability, and low density [10,22–24]. CTFs are also rich in nitrogen, which can produce interesting heteroatom effects and provide active sites for iodine adsorption [25]. Moreover, its large specific surface area and pore structure are important factors to promote iodine adsorption [26]. Jiang et al.[27] reported a new conjugated amorphous CTF-CTTD with an iodine adsorption capacity of up to 387 %. Previous studies have demonstrated that the unique conjugated rigid structure of CTFs can exhibit certain fluorescence and the fluorescence intensity will change after binding with metal ions, which can be used as a fluorescent probe for detecting metal ions. Hong et al.[28]synthesized a triazine conjugated porous organic polymer containing anthracic (DPA-CTF), which exhibits high sensitivity for Fe3+ ions. Therefore, the selection of CTFs as fluorescent probes for the detection of ferric ions and simultaneous use for iodine adsorption is a prospective initiative.

CTFs containing only N atoms have certain application limitations. With the development of research, the introduction of S-containing groups can significantly improve the application in the detection and adsorption of pollutants. Wang et al. [29]successfully polymerized a CTFs-based adsorbent material (CTF-CAR) for the adsorption treatment of rhodamine B and iodine vapor with maximum adsorption capacity of 199.70 mg/g and 2.86 g g−1, respectively. The polymer employs carbazole as an electron-rich central unit doped with S-containing thiophene groups. The polymer was used as the central unit for the adsorption of rhodamine B and iodine vapor. He et al.[30]synthesized a porous material (TSP-NS) with bi-functional triazine (containing N) and thiophene (containing S) using a one-step Friedel-Crafts reaction. Copper ions coordinate with the N and S heteroatoms in TSP-NS to form coordination complexes. So, this polymer is used for adsorption of copper ions. By analyzing the structure of the above polymers, it was found that the CTFs increased the content of heteroatoms after the introduction of thiophene groups containing S. However, its conjugated rigid structure is still insufficient, so the fluorescence response to metal ions is poorly performed. Therefore, this article will design and synthesize with strong conjugated rigid structure based on introducing S-containing groups, so that it is expected to achieve excellent performance of iron ion fluorescence detection and iodine adsorption at the same time.

In summary, this work uses 5,5′ −dibromo-2,2′ −bithiophene as the core, introduces the aldehyde group to the bithiophene through substitution reaction in the presence of tetra-(triphenylphosphine) palladium catalyst, and then undergoes catalyst-free Schiff base reaction with melamine to obtain rigid porous organic polymer CTF-DB-MA. CTF-DB-MA has eminent fluorescence in 1,4-dioxane solution, which can selectively detect Fe3+ ions. Besides, the iodine uptake performance of CTF-DB-MA was investigated by the capture of volatile iodine and iodine in solution. The maximum adsorption of iodine in saturated iodine vapor and iodine n-hexane solution by CTF-DB-MA was 2255.81 mg/g and 230.09 mg/g, respectively, and adequate recyclability. The structure, detection, adsorption and mechanism of the polymer are described in detail in this paper.

5,5′ −dibromo-2,2′ −bithiophene (DB), Pinacol 4-methoxycarbonyl phenyl borate, 1, 4-dioxane (Diox), potassium tert-butoxide, tetraphenyl phosphorus (Palladium), melamine (MA), dichloromethane (DCM), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), petroleum ether, acetone, toluene. These reagents and raw materials were purchased from the Energy Chemistry website and are pure enough to be used directly. The synthesis scheme of the intermediate CTF-DB is shown in S1.

The synthetic route of CTF-DB-MA was shown in Scheme 1 . The intermediates CTF-DB (1 g, 2.30 mmol) and melamine (0.29 g, 2.29 mmol) were weighed into a 100 mL round-bottomed flask, injected with 40 mL of DMSO, slowly stirred under argon protection, and then kept at 180 °C and heated to reflux for 72 h. During the reaction, beige solid precipitate gradually appeared. After the reaction was stopped, the temperature was allowed to cool to room temperature, the precipitate was removed and washed with water, filtered and dried, then extracted with dichloromethane, acetone/toluene (1:1) Soxhlet for 48 h. Finally, the target molecule CTF-DB-MA (2.76 g, 85 % yield) was obtained by vacuum drying at 80 ℃ for 24 h.

The chemical structure of DB-MA was determined using 1H NMR (Fig. S1). CTF-DB-MA introduces the melamine heteroatom N on the basis of CTF-DB, which gives it a more rigid and conjugated structure. CTF-DB-MA is insoluble in DMF, DMSO, THF, CHCl3 and other common organic solvents. It is easy to synthesized, and the synthesis conditions are mild and low cost.

The underlying regular arrangement and crystal structure of the polymer CTF-DB-MA backbone were analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction. In the PXRD spectrum (Fig. S2), a wide envelope peak appears at about 2θ = 22°, and CTF-DB-MA has no obvious and clear diffraction peak with crystal structure characteristics, which proves that CTF-DB-MA has an amorphous structure inside, that is, an amorphous type, which is consistent with the characteristics of most porous organic polymers.

In order to further determine the structural properties of the polymers, we characterized the chemical structures of the polymers using FT-IR spectroscopy and 13C CP-MAS. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1 a), the CO stretching absorption peak at 1722.12 cm−1 indicated that CTF-DB containing aldehyde group was successfully synthesized. Subsequently, the aldehyde group on CTF-DB undergoes an amine-formaldehyde condensation reaction with the amino group on melamine, resulting in the disappearance of the CO peak at 1722.12 cm−1, and the appearance of the CN and C-N bonds of the triazine ring at 1481.06 cm−1 and 1346.07 cm−1, respectively. The structure was further analyzed using 13C CP-MAS solid-state NMR spectroscopy (Fig. S3). Characteristic peaks appeared at 54.16, 125.35, 130.19, 137.62, 156.29 and 166.09 ppm, in which the resonance signal at 54.16 ppm represented the methoxy group, and the characteristic peak of the carbon atom in the triazine ring was located at 166.09 ppm. The peak at 156.29 ppm is a characteristic CN peak formed by condensation of aldehydes and amines. Other than that, the other characteristic peaks correspond to the positions of the characteristic peaks of the carbon atoms in the polymer structure. 13C NMR and FTIR spectroscopy provided sufficient information to determine the chemical structure and whether the reaction was successful. In this experiment, the polymer CTF-DB-MA was successfully synthesized by amine-formaldehyde condensation and catalyst-free Schiff base reaction.

In addition, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed at N2 as a way to observe the thermal stability of CTF-DB-MA (Fig. 1b). The material has a rapid thermal decomposition at about 245 ℃, and the weight is reduced at a faster rate. When the temperature is close to about 340 ℃, the internal network structure of CTF-DB-MA completely breaks down. As the temperature continues to rise, the material enters a slow decomposition phase, probably because the polymer contains small amounts of impurities. When the temperature reaches 510 ℃, the weight of the material does not change and reaches a stable state. This shows that CTF-DB-MA has excellent high-temperature resistance, which lays a good foundation for the subsequent experiments and is more widely used in production applications.

In order to understand the pore structure characteristics of CTF-DB-MA, the adsorption and desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of CTF-DB-MA samples were determined at 77 K. The specific surface area sizes and pore characteristics were also investigated. According to the classification criteria proposed by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the N2 adsorption–desorption curve of CTF-DB-MA (Fig. 1c) conforms to the form of the characteristic curve of category II. The specific surface area of CTF-DB-MA was determined to be 181.82 m2 g−1 based on the BET equation. The pore size distribution curve of CTF-DB-MA sample (Fig. 1d) shows that the pore size is mainly distributed between 5 and 20 nm, so CTF-DB-MA belongs to mesoporous materials. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize the morphology of CTF-DB-MA. The images showed that CTF-DB-MA appeared as irregular spherical polymeric particles and obvious pores could be observed (Fig. 1e). CTF-DB-MA is mainly dominated by mesoporous structures. These micro- and mesoporous structures demonstrate the presence of layered pores, which are useful for subsequent fluorescence detection and iodine adsorption experiments.

To select suitable dispersion solvent for sensing, CTF-DB-MA was dispersed in 1, 4-dioxane (Diox), methanol (MeOH), trichloromethane (CHCl3), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetonitrile (ACN), dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone (ACE) and ethanol (EtOH), respectively, their emission spectra were measured. The highest fluorescence peaks were observed for polymers dispersed in dioxane compared to other solvents (Fig. S4). Considering their luminescence intensity in different solvents, Diox was chosen as the dispersion medium for subsequent sensing experiments.

Considering that CTF-DB-MA has a large π conjugacy and is rich in N and S heteroatoms, which can coordinate with metal ions. Therefore, the potential of CTF-DB-MA for metal ion sensing was investigated. To the 1,4-dioxane dispersion of CTF-DB-MA were added equal amounts and concentrations (1 × 10−2 mol L−1) of different metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+). After adding different metal ions, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon appeared in the dispersion solution, which was manifested as the reduction of fluorescence intensity in different degrees (Fig. 2 a). Compared with the addition of other metal ions, the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on CTF-DB-MA was the most obvious, indicating that CTF-DB-MA has the best selectivity for Fe3+. We consider that this is mainly due to the fact that CTF-DB-MA has a more suitable pore size for Fe3+, which is favorable for binding with Fe3+.Moreover, the π-conjugated expansion of CTF-DB-MA and the enrichment of N and S active sites make Fe3+ complex with S atom (phenothiazine) and N atom (triazine ring)[31]. Based on the above factors, CTF-DB-MA shows good fluorescence sensing performance for Fe3+, so CTF-DB-MA can be used for the specific detection of Fe3+.

Good interference immunity is an important indicator for evaluating the usefulness of CTF-DB-MA. Fe3+ was mixed with the remaining metal ions (1 × 10−2 mol L−1, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) in equal amounts into a Diox solution, and 1 mL of CTF-DB-MA. sonicate for uniform dispersion. As shown in Fig. 2b, when Fe3+ coexists with other ions, the fluorescence intensity of CTF-DB-MA has little difference from that when only Fe3+ exists, indicating that CTF-DB-MA has excellent anti-interference performance for detecting Fe3+. From these results, it can be concluded that CTF-DB-MA can be a reliable fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+.

Next, CTF-DB-MA was subjected to a sensitivity test for Fe3+. A series of Fe3+ solutions and suspensions of CTF-DB-MA in 1,4-dioxane were prepared and titration experiments by fluorescence spectroscopy were performed. From Fig. 2c, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity tends to decrease with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, and within a certain range, there is a certain linear relationship between Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2d). The linear equation was determined to be y = -357x + 8515.31, and the R2 value was 0.99.

The limit of detection can be calculated according to Eq. (3–1) [32,33]:
(3-1)
L O D =
3 δ
S
Where LOD (mol/L) is the limit of detection, δ (mol/L) is the standard deviation of 20 blank probe sample measurements, and S = 357. The slope calculation formula of linear fitting curve in fluorescence spectral titration experiment is shown in Eq. (3–2) [34]:
(3-2)
δ =
∑
t = 1
N
F t
-
F
¯
2
N - 1
The LOD for Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.25 μM for polymer CTF-DB-MA (Table S1).

In order to understand the mechanism of Fe3+ induced fluorescence quenching of CTF-DB-MA, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on the Fe3+ test material (representing CTF-DBMA@Fe3+). As can be seen from Fig. 3 a, in addition to N and S elements, the XPS spectrum of CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+ also shows Fe3+ signal with a binding energy of 710.30 eV [35], indicating that the pores of CTF-DB-MA contain Fe3+. In addition, the Fe 2p XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA (Fig. 3b) showed Fe 2p3/2 for Fe2+ and Fe 2p1/2 for Fe3+ with binding energies of 724.44 eV and 710.22 eV, respectively [36]. This indicates that there may be charge transfer between Fe3+ and CTF-DB-MA. Compared with Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d, the binding energies of 399.20 eV and 397.90 eV formed by CN and C-N nitrogen atoms slightly increased in CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+. Increased to 399.29 eV and 398.07 eV, respectively [37,38], indicating that there is an interaction between Fe3+ positive ion and imide site in CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+. In addition, compared with Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f of S 2p XPS spectra, the binding energy of C-S-C increases from 168.68 eV to 169.04 eV in CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+, indicating the interaction between Fe3+ cation and C-S-C site in CTF-DB-MA@Fe3+. The spin doublet in the spectrum of Fig. 3f is S 2p1/2(165.02 eV) and S 2p3/2(160.46 eV). Corresponding to S 2p1/2(165.39 eV) and S 2p3/2(164.48 eV) in the spectrum of Fig. 3e [39,40], the binding energy is decreased.

The cyclic performance of CTF-DB-MA for iodine vapor adsorption was also investigated. To test the repeatability of CTF-DB-MA for iodine vapor, I2@CTF-DB-MA was washed several times with ethanol and dried under vacuum for one day and then used for subsequent replicate experiments. The results (Fig. 4b) showed that the maximum adsorption in the second cycle was 2180 mg/g, a decrease from 2250 mg/g in the first cycle. After several cycles, the amount of iodine adsorbed decreased, eventually to only 1870 mg/g after 5 cycles. The good cycle performance brings more possibilities to put CTF-DB-MA into practical applications in the future.

CTF-DB-MA has a large specific surface area and abundant pore structure, as well as ample electron-rich affinity sites and heteroatomic sites, which provide more space for the attachment of iodine in hexane solution. Therefore, exploratory experiments were performed on the equilibrium adsorption of iodine solutions (Fig. S6). It can be seen from Fig. 5 a that the maximum adsorption of iodine in hexane solution by CTF-DB-MA was 230.09 mg/g, and this figure exceeds some of the reported adsorbent materials to some extent (Table S3). Meanwhile, with the increasing adsorption time, the color of the solution gradually changed from purplish red to light color, and finally became basically colorless (Fig. 5b). A high level of iodine removal from the solution (77 %) was achieved in only 10 min. The equilibrium state was reached in about 240 min, and a high removal rate of 94.57 % was obtained. The above experimental results clearly show that CTF-DB-MA can quickly adsorb iodine in n-hexane solution with strong removal efficiency, which is very valuable for further improvement of the polymer structure for enhancing the removal of iodine in n-hexane solution.

The cyclic performance of CTF-DB-MA for adsorption of iodine from iodine hexane solution was investigated. I3 −@CTF-DB-MA was washed several times with ethanol, vacuum dried for one day, and then used for subsequent replicate experiments. The results (Fig. S7) showed that the maximum adsorption in the second cycle was 219.10 mg/g, which was decreased from 225.02 mg/g in the first cycle. After several cycles, the adsorbed amount of iodine gradually decreased, and after five cycles, the adsorbed amount of iodine was still 199.10 mg/g. CTF-DB-MA has good reusability in adsorption of iodine from iodine-hexane solution.

In addition, the isothermal adsorption of iodine by CTF-DB-MA in n-hexane solution was simulated on computer using Langmuir and Freundlich models, respectively. The fitting curve is shown in Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d respectively. From Table 2 , it is easy to see that the adsorption process of iodine in n-hexane by CTF-DB-MA is better fitted with the Langmuir adsorption model. Analyzing the results of Langmuir adsorption isotherm data, it can be concluded that the theoretical maximum iodine adsorption capacity of CTF-DB-MA is 259.84 mg/g. The discrepancy between the experimentally derived maximum adsorption amount and the fitted ideal maximum adsorption amount may be due to insufficient π-π interactions between the polymer and the iodine molecules during actual adsorption.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the polymer showed a distinctive I 3d peak after the adsorption of iodine, indicating the successful adsorption of iodine by CTF-DB-MA. As seen in the high-resolution XPS spectra of CTF-DB-MA N 1 s (Fig. 3c), CN and C-N peaks appeared at both 399.20 eV and 397.90 eV. Upon attachment of iodine vapor to CTF-DB-MA, the characteristic peaks of CN and C-N were significantly shifted to higher binding energies of 399.40 eV (CN) and 398.07 eV (C-N), respectively (Fig. 7 a) [42]. The results show that iodine interacts with the triazine ring of CTF-DB-MA. Upon adsorption of I3 − solution, the two peaks of CN and C-N in the triazine ring were converted to higher binding energies of 399.81 eV (CN) and 398.17 eV (C-N), respectively (Fig. 7b) [43–45].

Three peaks at 168.68 eV, 165.39 eV, and 164.48 eV were observed in the high-resolution XPS spectra of the S 2p of CTF-DB-MA (Fig. 3e). They are classified as C-S-C, S 2p1/2, and S 2p3/2, respectively [43–45]. After iodine vapor adsorption on CTF-DB-MA, the characteristic peaks of C-S-C, S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 shifted slightly towards higher binding energy. They are 168.94 eV(C-S-C), 165.42 eV (S 2p1/2), and 164.55 eV (S 2p3/2), respectively [46] (Fig. 7c). The results show that iodine interacts with thiophene groups of CTF-DB-MA. The three peaks of C-S-C, S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 in the thiophene group were converted to lower binding energies 168.51 eV(C-S-C), 164.02 eV (S 2p1/2) and 159.82 eV (S 2p3/2), respectively, after adsorption of I3 − in aqueous solutions [46–48] (Fig. 7d).

In the I 3d high-resolution spectrum of I2@CTF-DB-MA (Fig. 7e), two prominent peaks corresponding to the I 3d3/2 and I 3d5/2 orbitals were observed at 630.56 eV and 619.02 eV, in addition to the binding energies at 632.32 eV and 620.76 eV belonging to the I3 − and I5 − species, respectively [49]. The results suggest that some of the I2 is converted to I3 − and I5 − during the polymer adsorption of iodine. In the XPS spectra of I3 −@CTF-DB-MA (Fig. 7f), the I3 − signals appeared at binding energies 629.68 eV and 618.38 eV, while the I5 − signals appeared at binding energies 629.08 eV and 617.48 eV [50]. In conclusion, CTF-DB-MA adsorbs iodine vapor and I3 − through the interaction of the triazine ring and the heteroatoms N and S in the thiophene molecule with iodine.

In summary, the aim of this study is to synthesize bi-functional triazine polymers that can be used to detect Fe3+ ions and capture iodine. Compared with CTFs which only introduce heteroatom S, CTF-DB-MA has a strong conjugated rigid structure, which can not only adsorb iodine, but also be used for fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions. In Diox solution, CTF-DB-MA has a specific fluorescence sensing performance for Fe3+, and the LOD is 0.25 μM, which can be used as a Fe3+ detection material in a certain concentration range. In addition, CTF-DB-MA possessed excellent iodine adsorption capacity, as evidenced by the maximum adsorption of iodine in saturated iodine vapor and iodine n-hexane solution of 2255.81 mg/g and 230.09 mg/g, respectively. The cyclic experiments show that CTF-DB-MA can be effectively recovered after five consecutive iodine adsorption–desorption cycles. This excellent performance is mainly attributed to the conjugated rigid structure of CTF-DB-MA and the presence of heteroatoms N and S, and the large specific surface area, which makes it better able to produce adsorption and complexation. We expect the material to be widely used in environmental protection, and also provide some ideas for the follow-up research.

Ruihan Tang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Na Qiu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xiangfei Kong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenguang Hu: Supervision. Fuxin Zhong: Writing – review & editing. Peipei Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Haijun Tan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All authors acknowledge the financial support of this work from the Guangxi Science and Technology Development Program (AB2410051), Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magnetochemically Functional Materials (EMFM20241110).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Instrumentation and methods, Synthesis details, and 1H NMR spectra, solid NMR spectra, XRD spectra, etc.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2025.02.005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
